item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As soon as I saw the picture I started laughing my ass off because I never expected anyone to use SPIE proceedings to make an SCP

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Awesome! Will add when that timer expires if you don't beat me to the punch.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Ok, potential additions:

+ show block
– hide block

Subject matter: Psychiatry
Publication date: June 1976
Example article: A review and meta-analysis of 125 studies of "extrasensory perception" using chemical intervention, neurosurgery, or induced trauma.
Findings: Flagged for similarity to SCP-2498, SCP-2664, and [DATA EXPUNGED]. Six studies claimed weak evidence of anomalous mental phenomena, but the authors attributed this to normal statistical error. Most methods produced especially high casualty rates in children.

Subject matter: Forensic Biology
Publication date: July 2008
Example article: The ability to physically or mentally distinguish between identical twins.
Findings: Flagged for similarity to procedures 221-Crenshaw and 198-Cardinal. Researchers proceeded to vivisect or autopsy all subjects regardless of any differences detected.

Subject matter: Climate-Change Ecology
Publication date: September 2012
Example article: Potential human adaptations for survival in an amphibious environment.
Findings: Flagged for similarity to SCP-2235. All subjects expired from infection, complications from surgery, or forced drowning.

I could write more if there are any suggestions. Pretty much anything unethical is game on my end.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'd say the links to 2235, 2664, and the Kaktus proposal would best be left at your discretion, as I can promise you'd fit those into this article much more organically than I could.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Neat!

The sample magazine contents section could also be extended to 'mimic' the methods of terrible experiments taking place in other SCPs, just without the results. Osanshouo/SCP-2235 comes to mind, as well as SCP-2664, Kaktus's proposal, anything with a high amount of casualties or human cruelty. That might just end up spoiling the links, though.

There's also the potential to link the IJAMEA in more directly through the Epidemic Prevention and Water Purification Department. That said, it should definitely be carefully discussed with one or more of the primary authors in that canon, since that would be tying their creation to actual historic war crimes.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a few potential links here. First off, the mention of MTF Alpha-4 can be linked to the Pony Express entry on the MTF page. Second, the article mentions in the containment procedures that 2177 has been spotted at Site-11, which would make me want to move text based SCP objects based at that facility, such as SCP-986, to prevent potential contamination. Finally, the mode in which this pathogen spreads is similar to both SCP-1866 and SCP-2976, which suggests a potential of similar operation or origin and would warrant investigation. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

STRIX intelligence packages embedded in library collections management software are to monitor institutions for titles associated with SCP-2177 infection. Infections have been identified in 17 institutions, including ██ academic libraries, █ private collections, and the former non-classified public library of Site 11.

To this:

STRIX intelligence packages embedded in library collections management software are to monitor institutions for titles associated with SCP-2177 infection. Infections have been identified in 17 institutions, including ██ academic libraries, █ private collections, and the former non-classified public library of Site 111.

Changing this:

In order to avoid Beta events, Regional Directors have been authorized to provide financial and political support required to keep infected institutions in operation. If a Beta event is unavoidable, Mobile Task Force Alpha-4 ("Pony Express") is to intercept all deliveries of documents to vulnerable institutions and seize SCP-2177-1 instances until such time as a suitable institution can be infiltrated and infected.

To this:

In order to avoid Beta events, Regional Directors have been authorized to provide financial and political support required to keep infected institutions in operation. If a Beta event is unavoidable, Mobile Task Force Alpha-4 ("Pony Express") is to intercept all deliveries of documents to vulnerable institutions and seize SCP-2177-1 instances until such time as a suitable institution can be infiltrated and infected.

Changing this:

SCP-2177 is a virus-like pathogen that targets libraries holding print subscriptions to large numbers (> 50) of technical journals. SCP-2177 takes the form of documentation requesting that a journal or document be added to the institution’s collection. This request is always in the correct format for the receiving institution, and occasionally includes misinformation suggesting a pre-existing record. Once SCP-2177 has been entered into the catalog in any capacity, its record is altered to indicate an ongoing subscription to a technical journal. This facilitates reproduction, with new instances of SCP-2177-1 arriving amongst existing subscriptions. Reproduction rates range from 1 item every three months to 1 item per month.

To this:

SCP-2177 is a virus-like pathogen that targets libraries holding print subscriptions to large numbers (> 50) of technical journals. SCP-2177 takes the form of documentation requesting that a journal or document be added to the institution’s collection. This request is always in the correct format for the receiving institution, and occasionally includes misinformation suggesting a pre-existing record. Once SCP-2177 has been entered into the catalog in any capacity, its record is altered to indicate an ongoing subscription to a technical journal. This facilitates reproduction, with new instances of SCP-2177-1 arriving amongst existing subscriptions. Reproduction rates range from 1 item every three months to 1 item per month2.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Text based SCP objects at this site, including SCP-986 have had their containment procedures updated to prevent potential infection by SCP-2177
2. Similarity between the vectors of transmission, and contagious nature of SCP-2177, SCP-1866, and SCP-2976 has been noted. Investigation into a potential shared origin for these phenomena is ongoing.

item.title: Re: Yellow Journalism...
item.content:encoded: It's actually the University of Alabama in Huntsville. I think the furniture is kind of standardized.

item.title: Re: Yellow Journalism...
item.content:encoded:
is that the auburn library?
Also sorry bout bringing this thread back to life lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate it. I thought about including more detail about what exactly happens, like the changes in the catalog or how new journals show up in shipments, but the article was already running long and my ideas there just seemed less interesting than the other stuff to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to like this, but unfortunately I don't completely understand what the actual effect of the thing is, likely due to the novel way in which the phenomenon is described (don't be afraid to experiment with presentation, though!). No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+infinity for being exactly as grimdark as I was thinking it was.

+ I was also mentally linking in
- I was also mentally linking in

the unknown entities interpreting the use of the atom bomb as publicity stunt experiments by the US to recruit the scientists from Unit 731 and Project Paperclip.

Basically, they saw all of WWII as rival research groups trying to woo scientists away from each other by giving them unlimited budgets and no ethics restrictions.

Ergo, trying to do the same thing by showing their own portfolio of "exotic" research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

I agree about AA. I think it ultimately served to develop the climax very well, but it was a relatively minor objection I had to check-box along the way when getting into the world-building.

It was similar to my feelings on his frequent, mostly out-of-context Lovecraftian name-dropping in the first book. That normally turns me off in modern horror, but he explained in the afterward (I think) that he wasn't aware of the Call of Cthulhu rpg and the resurgence of Lovecraft, so he thought he was being clever.

I think it was much better done in the Fuller Memorandum, although that might just be because it was a more obscure historical figure and a much more plot-centric final result.

+ show block
– hide block

(and I love how it played out in the Apocalypse Codex).

Mind you, as much as I can complain, I love these books and Stross is one of my favorite authors. I like the Jennifer Morgue and the Fuller Memorandum best so far.

+ show block
– hide block

When I twigged to what was going on in Morgue, I basically flipped my shit.


I haven't read Rhesus Chart yet :( It isn't out in paperback for a while, and it was like $15 for a while on Kindle I think. I really need to bite the bullet and buy it.

(I'm enough of a nerd that I used to get relatives in the UK to ship me trade paperbacks, which came out there before our hardcovers do. No luck on this one, though.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hah! A fellow Laundry fan. Did you read Rhesus Chart yet, perchance? (I'm kinda at a loss for people to talk about that one yet XD)

TBH, I don't mind what he does in AA - for one, it's one of the rare cases where it actually fits well and doesn't paint the fascists in a better light - I mean, it'd have been tasteless if it was them doing it to save the world or something, but [PLOT REDACTED] doesn't really do that.

Also, it's not really an unique thing among his work - have a look at Fuller Memorandum and the reinterpretation of baron Roman von Ungern-Sternberg, who, put succintly, wasn't a nice guy in the least. In both cases, he manages to fit in with the real material really really well.

On an unrelated note +1 for this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Even more stuff!
- close

Man, the second one is way better than what I had in mind, and makes more sense with the findings in the additional entries. I think I'm gonna call that canon.

My initial idea was that the Society are scientists from "somewhere else" who may or may not be human and don't have the same kind of filters we do on what constitutes reasonable, ethical, or really even worthwhile research. When we made the immunity deal for Unit 731's data (and Operation Paperclip), they somehow got the impression that the US not only condoned that kind of research, but was in the market for more. The yellow journals/documentation are just how their communication comes out on our end.

- close

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks :) Actually no, although the Laundry Files is one of my favorite series.

All the library details are lifted from my old job, and the main inspiration was the thousands of actual yellow-bound conference proceedings we had to deal with. They were a pain in the ass to shelve/move, new ones came in all the time, literally nobody ever read them (because we had digital versions of all of them), but we weren't allowed to get rid of them.

The historical context came later.

Complete tangent: Although I loved the Atrocity Archives and I think Stross pulled it off rather well, I have mixed feelings about anything that tries to attribute a direct mystical or paranormal motivation for atrocities committed in WWII. I like to think that this one is more about unintended consequences than blood magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this inspired by the Atrocity Archives? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, but I can see two different interpretations, and I wanna know which is closer to the truth…

+Just guessin'
- 

Either the Society for Advanced Human Research is genuinely continuing to undertake these horrifying tests, and the infectious technical documentation is just a side effect of whatever they unleashed after the war, or…

They created this as a condemnation of US treatment of Unit 731, and made it a pretty damn tenacious condemnation at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anyone would like to know:

+ show block
– hide block

During the American occupation of Japan after WWII, General Douglass MacArthur (who was in charge of the occupation) arranged for legal immunity for members of Unit 731 (wiki link), Japan's bio- and chemical-warfare operation in occupied China. They didn't face war crimes trials for killing tens of thousands of people, and in exchange America got access to their research documents and the Soviets didn't.

I almost went with Operation Paperclip in honor of my home town's patron saint, Wernher Von Braun, but 731 made more sense.

– hide block

Don't know why I'm being coy about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting and well written, all you can ask for in an article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very impressive. The article does an excellent job of weaving a narrative while simultaneously outlining the parameters of an anomaly—something many authors struggle with. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it. Ur comment makes me confused on this issue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're not saying all the things that I think you're not saying in Document 2177-1947-01, you win at historical references.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice and somewhat simple. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent first SCP. Love the concept, the historical background and the execution.

item.title: Yellow Journalism...
item.content:encoded:
Hello folks! My first shot at an SCP. Thanks to Eskobar (and a few RL friends) for looking it over.

Pic is from my undergrad job. We really, really hated these things.
